behaviors.description: Logs with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them
behaviors.justification: This is pure data-processing logic: take an array of structured objects and group by a key field. No I/O, external calls, or timing dependencies. The failure mode (incorrect grouping) is entirely testable by passing test data through the function.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: create log entries with mixed trace IDs from different microservices, call the grouping function, verify output groups contain all entries and are indexed correctly by trace ID. Include cases with overlapping trace IDs across services.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services
behaviors.justification: Ordering logic based on timestamps is a pure algorithm with no I/O or external calls. Clock skew is handled via algorithm logic (grace period, causality constraints, or sliding window). Real failure mode is incorrect ordering given skewed timestamps, entirely testable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: create log entries with specific timestamp values including 500ms clock skew from different services, call ordering function, verify entries are ordered correctly or satisfy the skew-handling invariant (no causality violations within grace period).

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report
behaviors.justification: Anomaly detection is pure statistics on latency values: compute baseline percentile, compute p99, check threshold. No I/O, external calls, or non-determinism. Real failure modes (incorrect percentile calculation, wrong comparison) are testable with in-process data arrays.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: create latency datasets with known p99 and baseline values, call anomaly detector, verify spike is correctly flagged when p99 > 3x baseline and not flagged when below threshold. Include edge cases: uniform latencies, single outlier, empty data.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported
behaviors.justification: Core failure modes are: JSON parse error uncaught (crash) or skipped count incorrect. Both are testable by passing valid/invalid JSON to the parser and verifying error handling and counter logic. Unit testing the parser catches the core failures; Integration adds confidence but isn't the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: pass a mix of valid and invalid JSON strings to the parser, verify: (1) no uncaught exceptions are raised, (2) valid entries parse correctly, (3) invalid entries are marked as skipped, (4) skipped count equals the number of invalid entries. Test edge cases: truncated JSON, type mismatches, extra/missing fields.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind
behaviors.justification: Testing real throughput requires realistic I/O. Mocking I/O hides real bottlenecks (buffer management, kernel buffering, scheduling jitter, GC pauses). Dropping entries and queue buildup are OS-level phenomena. Only System testing with real data sources and concurrent load can verify the tool meets 10k/sec throughput without queue overflow or unbounded latency growth.
behaviors.test_description: System test: run the tool with a high-volume input stream (file, stdin, or network socket) generating ~10,000 entries/sec, measure processed count vs input count, measure end-to-end latency percentiles, verify no entry loss and latency does not grow unbounded. Include sustained load test (e.g., 60+ seconds at 10k/sec).
